note_data.title: Physical quantities and units
keyPoints: One radian is defined as the angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
A complete circle has an angle of 2π radians, allowing conversion between degrees and radians using π/180
Angular speed (ω) can be calculated as v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is time period and f is frequency
Objects moving in circular paths at constant speed have constantly changing velocity due to direction changes, requiring centripetal acceleration
Centripetal force always acts perpendicular to motion towards the center of the circle
Centripetal acceleration is calculated as a = v²/r = ω²r
Centripetal force is calculated as F = mv²/r = mω²r using Newton's second law
Gravitational fields are areas where objects with mass experience non-contact forces
Newton's law of gravitation states gravitational force is directly proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them: F = Gm₁m₂/r²
Gravitational field strength in radial fields follows g = GM/r², showing an inverse square relationship

definitions.definition: The angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length of that sector is equal to the radius of the circle
The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
The acceleration experienced by an object moving in a circular path at constant speed due to constantly changing velocity direction
The force that acts perpendicular to the motion of an object towards the center of the circle
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object
An area in which an object experiences a non-contact force

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the base unit or definition in one clear sentence without explanation
Give the reason why using 'because' - e.g. 'The equation is homogeneous because all terms have the same base units'
Work through the dimensional analysis step by step, showing all base units
State reasonable assumptions clearly, then calculate using simple numbers

modelAnswer.question: The equation v² = u² + 2as relates the motion of an object. Show that this equation is homogeneous. [3]
modelAnswer.answer:
LHS: v² has base units (m s⁻¹)² = m² s⁻²
First term on RHS: u² has base units (m s⁻¹)² = m² s⁻²
Second term on RHS: 2as has base units m s⁻² × m = m² s⁻²
All terms have the same base units (m² s⁻²), therefore the equation is homogeneous
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct base units for v² as m² s⁻²
Mark 2: Correct base units for both terms on RHS as m² s⁻²
Mark 3: Statement that equation is homogeneous because all terms have same base units

commonMistakes: Thinking objects in uniform circular motion are not accelerating because speed is constant - they are accelerating due to changing direction
Confusing the direction of centripetal force - it always acts towards the center of the circle, not outwards
Forgetting that gravitational field strength follows an inverse square relationship - doubling distance quarters the field strength
Not recognizing that mass of uniform spheres acts as point mass at center when calculating gravitational forces for objects outside the sphere
Mixing up angular speed formulas and not understanding the relationship between linear speed, angular speed and radius
examinerTips: Remember that centripetal force is the resultant force causing circular motion, not an additional force - identify what provides this force in each situation
When using gravitational formulas, ensure distances are measured between centers of masses, especially for spherical objects
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Table showing the seven base quantities (mass, length, time, electric current, thermodynamic temperature, amount of substance, luminous intensity) with their corresponding SI base units (kg, m, s, A, K, mol, cd) and common derived quantities
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q2 (3 marks) — they asked students to state the base quantities for force and energy, with many losing marks for writing 'weight' instead of mass
Featured in O/N 2022 P2 Q1 (4 marks) — candidates had to convert units from km/h to m/s and explain why consistent units are important in physics calculations
Appeared in M/J 2024 P1 Q3 (2 marks) — students needed to identify which quantities were vectors from a given list, with common errors including classifying speed as a vector
Tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — required measurement of length using appropriate instruments and discussion of uncertainty, with marks lost for incorrect significant figures

workedExample.question: A car travels 150 km in 2.5 hours. Calculate the average speed in m/s. Show all your working. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Write the speed formula: speed = distance ÷ time
Step 2: Convert distance to metres: 150 km = 150 × 1000 = 150,000 m
Step 3: Convert time to seconds: 2.5 hours = 2.5 × 3600 = 9000 s
Step 4: Calculate speed: 150,000 m ÷ 9000 s = 16.7 m/s (3 s.f.)
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for distance conversion, 1 mark for time conversion, 1 mark for final answer with appropriate significant figures

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused mass and weight units' — always use kg for mass and N for weight, never mix these up in calculations
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently omitted units in final answers' — always include the correct unit with your numerical answer, even if the question doesn't explicitly ask for it
Examiner Report 2022: 'Poor understanding of vector versus scalar quantities' — remember that vectors have both magnitude AND direction, scalars have magnitude only
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a clear table showing the seven base SI quantities (length-metre, mass-kilogram, time-second, electric current-ampere, temperature-kelvin, amount of substance-mole, luminous intensity-candela) with their symbols and units. Include a second table showing common derived quantities like velocity (m/s), acceleration (m/s²), and force (N = kg⋅m/s²).
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion requires centripetal force acting towards the center, calculated using F = mv²/r or F = mω²r. Angular measurements use radians where one radian equals arc length divided by radius. Gravitational fields exert forces on masses following Newton's law of gravitation F = Gm₁m₂/r². Field strength decreases with the inverse square of distance from the source mass.
